MORNING.TITLE: Morning
MORNING.TEXT_1: On a warm summer morning in the city, people are waking up and getting ready for their day. The Sun is already shining brightly.

VEHICLE_TYPES.TITLE: Vehicle Types
VEHICLE_TYPES.TEXT_1: People use many different types of vehicles to get to where they are going. What kind of vehicles might people use? Cars, buses, motorcycles, scooters, and bicycles... Most of these vehicles burn gasoline or natural gas, called fossil fuels, to make them go.

AIR_POLLUTION.TITLE: Air Pollution
AIR_POLLUTION.TEXT_1: Most of the gases we’re adding to the air, from things like burning fossil fuels or using industrial chemicals, are considered air pollution.
AIR_POLLUTION.TEXT_2: These gases make the air less clean, which is bad for our health.

HEALTH_IMPACTS.TITLE: Health Impacts
HEALTH_IMPACTS.TEXT_1: Air pollution can damage our lungs, and harm plants and animals, too.
HEALTH_IMPACTS.LUNGS_BEFORE: Healthy lungs have wide open pathways.
HEALTH_IMPACTS.LUNGS_AFTER: With high ground ozone levels, muscles contract, pathways for air are narrowed, and breathing is difficult.

SUNNY_DAY.TITLE: Sunny Day
SUNNY_DAY.TEXT_1: But on a warm sunny day when certain types of air pollution are already in the atmosphere, something else can form.

AIR.TITLE: Air
AIR.NARRATIVE: What's in the air around us?

NEARBY_FACTORIES.TITLE: Nearby Factories
NEARBY_FACTORIES.TEXT_1: Nearby factories also burn fossil fuels to make electricity that we use in our homes, schools, and workplaces. Click on the fossil fuels to see the electricity being produced!

FUEL_SOURCES.TITLE: Fuel Sources
FUEL_SOURCES.TEXT_1: [PLACEHOLDER TEXT] Click the fossil fuel source to learn more about where they are found!
FUEL_SOURCES.OIL_BUBBLE: [PLACEHOLDER] Oil is extracted from underground reservoirs
FUEL_SOURCES.COAL_BUBBLE: [PLACEHOLDER] Coal is found in deposits within sedimentary rock layers

BURNING_FUELS.TITLE: Burning Fuel
BURNING_FUELS.TEXT_1: Burning fossil fuels releases invisible gases into the air — carbon dioxide, nitrogen dioxide, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), and other gases, too. Factories and vehicles also release solid particles of dust, carbon soot, and smoke that are so tiny they can float in the air for a long time.
BURNING_FUELS.FACTORY_BUBBLE: We burn fossil fuels to create energy for the city!
BURNING_FUELS.CAR_BUBBLE: Engines burn gasoline to make cars move!

OZONE_INGREDIENTS.TITLE: Ozone Ingredients
OZONE_INGREDIENTS.TEXT_1: Let’s gather the atmospheric “ingredients” needed to make this particular type of air pollution. We need: VOCs, which can come from car exhaust pipes, or from chemicals in paint or other industrial products; we also need nitrogen oxides like NO₂, which also come from car exhaust pipes and from factory emissions.

GATHER_INGREDIENTS.TITLE: Gathering Ingredients
GATHER_INGREDIENTS.TEXT_1: Create ozone by dragging VOC and NO₂ molecules to the smog cloud.
GATHER_INGREDIENTS.INGREDIENTS_GATHERED_BUBBLE: Great! Now that you have gathered the ingredients you can procced

AREA.PROMPT: Ozone pollution is worse in certain areas. Can you guess where this happens?
AREA.CORRECT: Correct! Ozone pollution is worse in urban areas!
AREA.INCORRECT: Actually, no! There is less ozone pollution in rural areas!

SEASON.PROMPT: Another factor that influences ozone pollution is time of year. When might pollution be worse?
SEASON.CORRECT: Correct! Ozone pollution is worse in the hot and sunny months!
SEASON.INCORRECT: Actually, no! Cool and dark months will have less ozone pollution!

CARS_FACTORIES.PROMPT: Density of cars and factories also makes an impact since these emit the ingredients of ozone. Knowing this, where might pollution be worse?
CARS_FACTORIES.CHOICE_LOTS: Areas with lots of cars and factories!
CARS_FACTORIES.CHOICE_FEW: Areas with fewer cars or factories!
CARS_FACTORIES.CORRECT: Correct! Ozone pollution is worse where there are many cars and/or factories!
CARS_FACTORIES.INCORRECT: Actually, no! Cars and factories produce the ingredients for ozone, so pollution is worse where there are more of them!

SOLUTIONS.TITLE: Solutions
SOLUTIONS.PROMPT: There are many things we can do to reduce ozone pollution. Select the actions you would like to take to help out!

RENEWABLE_ENERGY.TITLE: Choose renewable energy.
RENEWABLE_ENERGY.DESC: Wind, solar, and other renewables make electricity without air pollution.

LOW_POLLUTION_VEHICLE.TITLE: Choose a low-pollution vehicle.
LOW_POLLUTION_VEHICLE.DESC: Fuel efficient cars and electric cars help keep air quality good.

IDLE_ENGINE.TITLE: Leave the engine running.
IDLE_ENGINE.DESC: Keep your car running while waiting to keep the engine warm.

LOW_VOC_PRODUCTS.TITLE: Choose low VOC products.
LOW_VOC_PRODUCTS.DESC: Choose paints, adhesives, household cleaners, and other products that have low or no VOCs.

LESS_ENERGY.TITLE: Use less energy.
LESS_ENERGY.DESC: This will reduce air pollution if your energy comes from gas or coal and save money.

BURN_TRASH.TITLE: Burn your trash.
BURN_TRASH.DESC: Get rid of waste quickly by burning it in your backyard.

WALK_BIKE_BUS.TITLE: Walk, bike, or take the bus.
WALK_BIKE_BUS.DESC: These are low-cost ways to get from place to place and combine errands for efficiency.

FORGO_FIRE.TITLE: Forgo the fire.
FORGO_FIRE.DESC: On days when air quality is poor, avoid using your wood fireplace or wood stove if you can.

MOW_MORNING.TITLE: Mow in the morning.
MOW_MORNING.DESC: Mow your lawn early in the day when it's cool outside.

COMPOST_YARD_WASTE.TITLE: Compost yard waste.
COMPOST_YARD_WASTE.DESC: Avoid burning leaves, branches, and other plant trimmings by composting them.

MOW_EVENING.TITLE: Mow in the evening.
MOW_EVENING.DESC: This helps prevent ozone formation. Or use an electric or manual mower.

REFUEL_EVENING.TITLE: Refuel in the evening.
REFUEL_EVENING.DESC: At gas stations, gasoline vapors get into the air, which help make ozone on hot, sunny days.

SOLUTIONS.CONCLUSION: Nice work! You've chosen {{count}} actions to take to help reduce ozone pollution.
SOLUTIONS.CONCLUSION_PERFECT: Fantastic! You chose {{correctCount}} helpful actions and didn't fall for any tricks!
SOLUTIONS.CONCLUSION_MIXED: Good job finding {{correctCount}} helpful actions! However, {{incorrectCount}} of your choices would actually make pollution worse.
SOLUTIONS.CONCLUSION_ALL_INCORRECT: Oh no! Those choices would actually create more ozone pollution. Let's be careful!

OZONE_MOLECULE.TITLE: How Ozone Forms
OZONE_MOLECULE.TEXT_1: Tap the Sun to release ultraviolet (UV) light.
OZONE_MOLECULE.TEXT_2: Ultraviolet light strikes a nitrogen dioxide molecule. Tap the NO₂ molecule.
OZONE_MOLECULE.TEXT_3: The NO₂ molecule absorbs the energy and splits, forming nitric oxide (NO) and releasing a free oxygen atom.
OZONE_MOLECULE.TEXT_4: Tap the free oxygen atom to move it toward the oxygen molecule (O₂).
OZONE_MOLECULE.TEXT_5: The oxygen atom joins the O₂ molecule to form ozone (O₃). Tap the ozone molecule to restart the reaction.

UPPER_OZONE.TITLE: Upper Atmosphere Ozone
UPPER_OZONE.TEXT_1: Now way up high in the atmosphere, ozone is naturally there in the ozone layer. Ozone up in the stratosphere is actually really helpful. It helps block UV rays from the Sun that can cause sunburn.

END.TITLE: End
END.TEXT_1: This is here for testing purposes
